passage:
Text 1
Urban parks have long been considered essential public spaces, but city planners increasingly debate whether their benefits justify their high maintenance costs. A 2022 survey of municipal budget directors found that parks consumed an average of 8% of city operating budgets, leading some officials to propose reducing parkland in favor of revenue-generating developments.

Text 2
A study published in the Journal of Urban Economics analyzed property tax records from 14 American cities. The researchers found that residential properties within 500 meters of a park commanded prices 11% higher than comparable properties farther away. In cities where parks occupied more than 10% of total land area, this premium rose to 18%. The researchers concluded that parks generate substantial tax revenue for municipalities through elevated property values.

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from urban planning policy literature

stem: How does the information in Text 2 relate to the debate described in Text 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It suggests parks may generate revenue rather than only consume it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It confirms that parks are too expensive for most city budgets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It shows that homeowners value parks more than city officials do.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It proves that reducing parkland would lower municipal budgets.

explanation: Text 1 frames parks as costly, with some officials arguing their 8% budget share is hard to justify. Text 2 directly counters this by showing that parks increase nearby property values (by 11-18%), which in turn raises property tax revenue for the city. Together, these sources reveal that parks may actually help pay for themselves through elevated tax receipts — meaning the cost-only framing in Text 1 is incomplete. Choice A accurately synthesizes this relationship.

distractorRationale.A: Correct. Text 2's data on property value premiums implies parks generate additional tax revenue, challenging Text 1's cost-focused framing.
distractorRationale.B: This misreads Text 2, which actually provides evidence against the idea that parks are purely a budget drain — it shows a financial benefit, not a confirmation of high costs.
distractorRationale.C: Neither text compares homeowner preferences to city officials' views. Text 2 reports market data on property prices, not attitudinal comparisons between groups.
distractorRationale.D: Text 2 shows the opposite: parks appear to increase municipal revenue through higher property values. Claiming park reduction would lower budgets inverts the logic of Text 2.
